head_coach.name: Mike Vrabel
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Physical, disciplined defense with disguised coverages and aggressive fronts; adaptable offense built around QB development. Former LB who played under Belichick and Cowher, bringing a hybrid Belichick defensive philosophy with more one-gap aggression. Led Patriots to Super Bowl LX appearance in Year 1 (14-3).

offensive_coordinator.name: Josh McDaniels
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Belichick/Erhardt-Perkins, with modern RPO and zone-run influences from Thomas Brown (McVay tree)

defensive_coordinator.name: Terrell Williams
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Vrabel/Belichick 3-4 one-gap aggressive front; DL coaching background with Titans (2018-2023) and Lions (2024)

offense.personnel_grouping_detail: Heavy personnel usage among the highest in the NFL. 21 personnel (2 RB, 1 TE, 2 WR) used at a top-3 rate league-wide; 12 personnel (1 RB, 2 TE, 2 WR) deployed on approximately 30% of snaps (12th in NFL). McDaniels is one of the most frequent deployers of heavy offensive sets in the league.
offense.run_scheme: Transitioning from traditional gap/power to more outside zone, but retaining ISO, power, counter, wham, and crack toss. Mix of zone and gap concepts with zone schemes becoming more prominent in 2025 to fit personnel (TreVeyon Henderson) and Drake Maye's mobility.
offense.pass_concepts: Play-action heavy off run-action, boot concepts sequenced off toss schemes, dropback passing from multiple formations, moving pockets to leverage Maye's athleticism. RPO elements added via Thomas Brown influence. Quick game and vertical shots off play-action.
offense.rpo_usage: moderate-to-high (increased from historical McDaniels norms, Thomas Brown influence bringing more early-down RPO elements)
offense.te_usage: Dual-role: inline blocker in heavy sets AND play-action target from 12-personnel. TEs run crossers off play-action, used in 2-TE sets frequently. Thomas Brown (McVay tree) serves as TE coach/pass-game coordinator, elevating TE receiving role. Heavy 12-personnel usage means TEs are integral to both run blocking and route running.
offense.rb_usage: Multiple-back system with heavy 21-personnel usage. Committee approach: TreVeyon Henderson as zone-scheme speed back, with fullback/H-back types for lead ISO. RBs used in pass game as part of play-action design. 2-RB sets force defenses to replace DBs with linebackers, creating mismatches downfield.

defense.alignment: Nominally 3-4 but operates functionally as a 4-3 under with one-gap responsibilities. Shifts to 4-down linemen on passing downs with overloads, wide-nine alignments, and 3-technique rushers.
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up primary (Harold Landry, K'Lavon Chaisson), with DEs rushing from 3-point stance on passing downs. OLB coach Mike Smith is specifically a pass-rush specialist who coached Danielle Hunter (16.5 sacks) and Dee Ford (13 sacks). System uses both 2-point and 3-point stance rushers.
defense.coverage_base: Multiple / disguise-heavy. Mix of Cover 3, Cover 1 (man-free), Cover 2, Tampa 2, Quarters, and inverted coverages. Heavy use of pre-snap disguise — showing one coverage and rotating to another post-snap. Man coverage tendency remains high. Creeper pressures at 5th-highest rate in NFL. Cover Zero usage at 3rd-highest rate (8%).
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate-to-heavy (heavy use of simulated/creeper pressures where DBs blitz and DL drops into coverage; 2nd-highest rate of unblocked pressures in NFL)
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Zak Kuhr coaching) are run/coverage players expected to run free behind DL that occupies blockers. OLBs (Mike Smith coaching) are primary edge pass rushers who also occasionally drop into coverage on creeper/sim pressure plays. System is designed to keep ILBs clean to flow to the ball.
defense.safety_usage: Hybrid-heavy. Dime personnel used at 3rd-highest rate in NFL (22.8%). Safety types like Jabrill Peppers, Kyle Dugger, and Craig Woodson play box, slot, and blitz roles. Dugger ranked 4th among DBs in QB pressures. Peppers tied for 4th in run stops among safeties. Safeties rotate pre-snap as part of coverage disguise scheme.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: The Patriots run 12-personnel at approximately 30% of snaps with Thomas Brown (Sean McVay coaching tree) serving as TE coach and pass-game coordinator — the first pass-game coordinator title in team history. McDaniels sequences play-action passes directly off heavy personnel looks, with TEs running crossers from 12-personnel as primary targets. The Erhardt-Perkins system historically values versatile TEs who can both block in-line and run routes from multiple alignments. A receiving TE who can also hold up as an in-line blocker in heavy sets has tremendous value here because the entire offensive structure relies on presenting heavy formations to force defensive personnel changes, then exploiting coverage mismatches in the passing game.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: The defense operates as a 3-4 with stand-up OLBs as primary edge rushers. OLB coach Mike Smith is a dedicated pass-rush specialist who coached Danielle Hunter to 16.5 sacks and Dee Ford to 13 sacks in his previous stops. Harold Landry (8.5 sacks) and K'Lavon Chaisson (7.5 sacks) were the team's top sack producers in 2025 as stand-up OLBs. Edge rusher is the team's most glaring need heading into 2026 — Patriots ranked 20th in sack percentage and 26th in total sacks. Any LB with elite pass-rush traits (burst, bend, hand usage) would be valued at EDGE prices because the system uses OLBs both in 2-point and 3-point stances as the primary source of edge pressure. Vrabel's Titans historically drafted OLBs in the 6-3, 250-260 pound range.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: McDaniels' 2025 offense uses 21 personnel at a top-3 rate league-wide. The heavy personnel groupings are specifically designed to force defenses to replace DBs with linebackers, creating passing mismatches. McDaniels then uses RBs in play-action concepts and occasionally as receivers out of the backfield. The system values backs who can catch and operate in space because the entire offensive strategy revolves around making heavy formations a passing threat. However, the primary RB value driver remains running ability (zone scheme fit, burst, vision) rather than pure receiving. A back with legitimate receiving chops gets a meaningful boost but it's secondary to running traits.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: The Patriots deploy dime personnel at the 3rd-highest rate in the NFL (22.8%), meaning they frequently have 6 DBs on the field. This is only possible because their safeties function as hybrid defenders — playing box, slot coverage, blitzing, and rotating through coverage disguises. Kyle Dugger ranked 4th among all DBs in QB pressures; Jabrill Peppers tied for 4th in run stops among safeties. The defense uses creeper pressures (5th-highest rate) and cover-zero (3rd-highest rate at 8%), both of which require safeties who can blitz, play man coverage, and handle the run. A safety with box/slot/blitz versatility is extremely valuable because the entire defensive philosophy relies on hybrid DBs who eliminate the need for traditional linebacker personnel.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: New England's 2026 draft needs align perfectly with several positional value inflections. EDGE/OLB is the team's most critical need: they ranked 20th in sack percentage and 26th in sacks in 2025, and K'Lavon Chaisson is hitting free agency. The 3-4 system with stand-up OLBs means any prospect with pass-rush traits — whether labeled LB or EDGE — gets valued at a premium. Vrabel's Titans historically drafted OLBs around 6-3/259 pounds who could rush from both 2-point and 3-point stances. On offense, McDaniels' heavy personnel philosophy (top-3 in 21 personnel, 30% 12 personnel) means TEs who can both block and catch have elevated value. Thomas Brown's McVay-tree influence as pass-game coordinator specifically elevates receiving TEs further. A TE like Kenyon Sadiq with elite receiving traits would be schematically maximized here because the offense uses TE-heavy formations as the foundation of its play-action passing attack. Safety is another high-value position: the defense's 3rd-highest dime rate in the NFL and reliance on creeper/sim pressures demand hybrid safeties who can cover, blitz, and play the run. Any safety prospect with box/slot versatility gets a significant boost. RBs who can contribute in the passing game get a moderate-to-high boost because McDaniels' heavy formations are specifically designed to create passing mismatches — but pure running ability remains the primary trait given the zone scheme transition.
